The word “treason” is back in circulation.  
Every time a senator sounds soft on a foreign power or parrots some external propaganda line, the comments fill up with one demand: tanggalin na ’yan.

People are angry, and they should be. But anger meets a wall the moment you ask a harder question: how, exactly, do you remove a sitting senator in this country?

🟥 WHAT TREASON FEELS LIKE VS WHAT THE LAW SAYS

In everyday language, “traitor” is wide. You can use it on anyone who sells you out.

In the law, treason is narrow, tiny, well-defined.

Article 114 of the Revised Penal Code says treason is committed by someone who owes allegiance to the Philippine government, during a war involving the Philippines, when that person either levies war against the government or adheres to its enemies by giving them aid or comfort. 

Commentaries on crimes against national security repeat the same thing: treason is essentially a war crime. 

In peacetime, the label doesn’t cleanly apply, no matter how much a politician reeks of foreign influence.

So when people online call a senator’s remarks “treasonous,” they’re almost always speaking morally and politically, not legally. 

They’re saying: you sound like you’re working for someone else. You sound captured. You sound like a tuta.

That has real weight in politics. But it doesn’t automatically unlock a criminal case.

🟥 HOW A SENATOR ACTUALLY LOSES THE SEAT

This is the first bitter pill: senators are difficult to remove by design.

They are not impeachable officials. 

Impeachment is for the President, Vice President, Supreme Court justices, members of constitutional commissions, and the Ombudsman. Senators are outside that list.

There is no recall election for senators. No mechanism where a petition, no matter how viral, directly forces a mid‑term vote to fire them.

What actually exists is this:

The Senate can punish, suspend, or expel a member for disorderly behavior if two‑thirds of all its members vote for it, under Article VI, Section 16(3). In a 24‑member Senate, that means sixteen colleagues have to decide that one of their own is no longer tolerable.

Courts can effectively take a senator out of circulation if they’re convicted of a serious crime, jailed, or disqualified. But even that takes time, evidence, and political will in the justice system.

If you recall, this is what the Rodrigo Duterte administration intentionally did to then Senator Leila Delima.

The rest is elections. If they survive everything, they face you again at the ballot box and gamble on your short memory.

So when people shout “treason, tanggalin,” the brutal truth is: removing them still runs through other powerful people. Not directly through you. 

Meaning, no matter how angry you get, you cannot remove a sitting senator.

🟥 THE ETHICS CASES THE SENATE WON’T TOUCH

Now park treason for a moment and look at what is already in front of the Senate, on paper.

There is an ethics complaint against Francis “Chiz” Escudero over campaign money. 

Lawyer Marvin Aceron says Escudero accepted around thirty to thirty‑five million pesos in campaign donations from contractor Lawrence Lubiano of Centerways Construction. 

Stories have traced Centerways’ contracts in Sorsogon, Escudero’s turf, and noted how its government projects climbed from 2021 onwards. 

Comelec later cleared Escudero and Lubiano in an election offense case, but the ethics angle is wider than the Omnibus Election Code. 

It asks: what does it mean when a senator is bankrolled by a contractor that also profits from public works in his backyard?

There is an ethics complaint against Risa Hontiveros, filed by Duterte‑aligned lawyers Ferdinand Topacio, Manuelito Luna, and Jacinto Paras. 

They accuse her of “witness tampering” in the Michael Maurrilo episode, where Maurrilo initially testified on alleged abuses linked to Apollo Quiboloy’s Kingdom of Jesus Christ, then later recanted. 

Hontiveros calls this harassment for doing oversight work. Her accusers call it unethical interference. The ethics panel has the complaint.

There is an ethics complaint against JV Ejercito, the ethics chair himself. Aceron alleges “gross neglect of duty” because Ejercito sat on the Escudero complaint for over 100 days without even assigning a docket number. 

The complaint asks for his censure, removal as chair, and inhibition from any case involving Escudero.

JV admits that, all in all, four to five senators now face ethics complaints. ANC and Bilyonaryo News Channel talk about “five senators” under ethics fire, but the committee still hasn’t released a full, official list with all names and accusations laid out together. Everything we know comes in fragments.

And when the Senate Committee on Ethics and Privileges finally convened its organizational meeting this February, ABS‑CBN’s headline told the whole story in one line: “Senate ethics panel convenes; no case taken up yet.” The committee organized itself. It did not touch a single pending complaint.

On paper, they have work. In reality, they’re choosing not to do it.

And the more you look at it, the more one pattern becomes obvious: the old guard in the Senate protects its own.

🟥 THE BLUE RIBBON REPORT THAT GREW A SPINE, THEN LOST IT

If you want a perfect snapshot of how the old guard works, look at the Senate Blue Ribbon Committee’s flood control investigation.

The Blue Ribbon, chaired by Panfilo Lacson, investigated alleged anomalies in flood control projects: overpricing, kickbacks, and contractors greasing politicians. 

Centerways and other firms appeared. Senators Chiz Escudero, Joel Villanueva, and Jinggoy Estrada, along with ex‑officials like Zaldy Co and Bong Revilla, were dragged into the story through testimonies and documents.

Lacson confirmed there was a draft committee report that recommended filing plunder and other criminal charges against Escudero, Villanueva, and Estrada, plus administrative and criminal raps against ex‑lawmakers and contractors. 

At least four senators initially signed it, including Risa Hontiveros, Bam Aquino, Kiko Pangilinan, and Lacson himself.

Then the draft leaked.

Suddenly, Senate President Tito Sotto was telling reporters that three members had withdrawn their signatures. 

Majority Leader Migz Zubiri, JV Ejercito, and Sherwin Gatchalian wrote Lacson to retract, saying they needed more time and that the version they signed was “for discussion” only. A “draft partial” report, not yet final.

The language in the new version turned softer. 

Earlier drafts used stronger phrasing like “recommended to be charged,” suggesting clear endorsement of filing cases. 

The revised draft softened it to “undergo preliminary investigation” or “fact‑finding” and “case build‑up” by the DOJ and Ombudsman. Lacson insisted the essence was the same, but newsrooms and even Justice Secretary Boying Remulla noted what everyone could see: the wording was adjusted to avoid directly telling law enforcers, “file charges now.”

This is the Senate in one scene: a draft that names colleagues and suggests plunder raps; a handful of senators willing, at first, to sign; a leak; a mini‑panic; signatures quietly withdrawn; language softened.

Not because evidence magically disappeared in a week. Because signatures have consequences.

🟥 THE OLD GUARD: PROTECTION, POWERPLAYS, CONCESSIONS

People love to describe the Senate as an “old boys’ club.” That’s accurate, but incomplete. Inside that club, survival comes first. Friendships are just the wrapping.

The two‑thirds vote requirement to suspend or expel is part of the shield. 

To punish one senator severely, sixteen others have to accept that what this person did is so wrong it justifies a precedent that might be used against them later. 

That’s a hard sell in a chamber where almost everyone has donors, deals, and decisions they’d rather not have weaponized.

Then there’s the public line that the ethics panel “must not be used as a weapon.” JV Ejercito keeps saying the committee should not be a political tool for people with “ulterior motives.” 

Everyone nods, because we’ve all seen bad‑faith complaints. But in practice, that line becomes a blanket excuse to slow‑walk anything that might hurt a friend, ally, or future running mate. 

If every complaint can be labeled “weaponized,” then anything politically inconvenient can be delayed for “prudence.”

The procedural fog adds another layer. 

The ethics panel has spent months talking about being “not yet fully constituted,” about members with conflicts of interest who had to be removed, about reviewing internal rules, about being busy with the budget, about adopting a “first in, first out” system. None of this is fake. 

But stretched over time, it becomes what it looks like from the outside: a polite, bureaucratic way to tell the public, huwag muna, may inaayos pa kami.

Underneath that, you have the real engine: powerplays, negotiations, concessions.

Committee chairmanships are bargaining chips. Investigations are bargaining chips. Silence is a bargaining chip. 

A senator can quietly agree not to push an ethics case in exchange for support on another committee, protection in a separate inquiry, or simple non‑retaliation when their own donor list shows up in someone else’s slideshow.

This is why even something like the Blue Ribbon report becomes a battlefield. 

Tell the 'truth' and you don't just make a point; you put your alliance on the line by looking like you signed a colleague's political death warrant.

So when you ask “why isn’t the ethics complaint moving?”, the answer is rarely just “they’re lazy.” It’s: the complaint is sitting in the middle of a network of favors and threats. Every step forward has a price.

A price no sitting senator is willing to pay in the name of survival.

🟥 THE HOUSE: KIKO BARZAGA, ARNIE TEVES, AND A VERY DIFFERENT INSTINCT

Then you turn your head to the lower House, the place we love to call trapo central, and you see a different reflex on discipline.

Cavite Rep. Kiko Barzaga became one example. Ethics complaints were filed over his lewd social media posts with scantily clad women and flashy displays of cash. 

The House ethics committee found him guilty of disorderly behavior and recommended a 60‑day suspension without salary and allowances. The plenary adopted it, with an overwhelming vote in favor.

When his behavior didn’t really change, the committee went back at him.  Fresh penalties were on the table, including another 60‑day suspension, which the House again backed. 

The message was simple: we may tolerate a lot, but there are lines you cannot cross in public without consequences.

Arnie Teves is the sharper case. He fled, refused to return, was linked to the Degamo killing, and later tagged as a terrorist. 

The House ethics committee recommended suspensions over his chronic absence and disorderly behavior. 

The plenary adopted them. When he still didn’t come back, they escalated. 

Eventually the House voted to expel him — the first “proper” expulsion under the 1987 Constitution, according to a law journal study.

Pantaleon Alvarez, for his part, was suspended over remarks calling on the military and police to withdraw support from Marcos — again, the House using ethics and discipline to declare some lines too far, even for their own.

The House has its own deep problems. No one’s romanticizing it. But on the narrow question of actually suspending and expelling, it has done what the Senate keeps refusing to do.

Why?

Because size and structure make sacrifice easier. 

The House has 300+ members. Losing one or two does not rearrange the entire power map. Leadership has tighter control over carrots and sticks. 

When someone becomes too embarrassing or too dangerous, throwing them to the wolves can even strengthen the institution’s façade.

The Senate, with twenty‑four personalities, future presidential bets, and national funders, sees every expulsion or major sanction as a mini‑earthquake. 

That’s why the Blue Ribbon signatures were so painful and why some were pulled back. 

That’s why the ethics committee spends weeks on its own rules instead of its own raps.

🟥 SO WHAT DO WE DO WITH “TREASONOUS SENATORS”?

If you’re angry at senators who sound like they’re working for someone else, you’re not wrong. But you need to know the terrain you’re standing on.

Legally, treason is almost impossible to use in peacetime.  
Institutionally, expulsion requires sixteen senators willing to say, “this one of us is beyond saving.”  
Politically, every ethics case is not just a question of facts, but a test of alliances, deals, and future ambitions.

The House has shown it can suspend Kiko Barzaga twice for disgusting behavior and expel Arnie Teves for abandoning his duties. 

The Senate has shown it can write a Blue Ribbon draft that dares to recommend plunder raps against Escudero, Villanueva, and Estrada, then watch some members quietly withdraw their signatures and soften the language when things get too real.

So when you say “treasonous” today, maybe it helps to be precise.

You’re not just saying a senator has betrayed the country. You’re saying:

They have made choices that, in a healthier system, would at least trigger serious ethics hearings or criminal investigations.

You’re also saying:

We live under a Senate that still believes protecting each other is safer than protecting the public trust.

Until that calculation flips — until the cost of shielding one another becomes higher than the cost of cleaning house — the ethics complaints will sit there, the Blue Ribbon drafts will keep getting “corrected,” and “treason” will stay a word we shout at the TV, not a line the system is brave enough to enforce.

🟥 SWS SURVEY ON SARA DUTERTE: MAKE IT MAKE SENSE  

I saw the latest Social Weather Stations survey results and I had to read them twice.  

Sara Duterte is facing impeachment. She's at the center of a P612.5 million confidential funds scandal. She's been accused of threatening to assassinate the President. She allegedly ordered subordinates to fabricate reports. And yet, her satisfaction rating climbed to +28 in November 2025. That's higher than President Marcos Jr., who sits at a neutral -3. In Mindanao, she posted +74.

I kept looking at the numbers, waiting for them to make sense. They didn't.  

🟥 THE TIMELINE THAT SHOULD HAVE BURIED HER  

Throughout 2025, Sara Duterte has been under constant pressure. The House impeached her in February 2025. The Supreme Court later declared those articles unconstitutional in July. 

Then in February 2026, new impeachment complaints landed, this time over betrayal of public trust, the confidential funds mess, and her own admission of contracting an assassin to kill Marcos, First Lady Liza, and then-Speaker Romualdez.

Any other politician would have seen their numbers collapse.  

Hers went up. From +22 in September to +28 in November.  

And Marcos? He went the opposite direction. His satisfaction rating slid from +10 in June to -3 by November.  

🟥 THE VICTIMHOOD PLAY: WHEN BEING ATTACKED BECOMES YOUR BEST WEAPON  

Here's what I think explains the numbers: Sara Duterte and her camp have turned every legal action against her into proof of persecution. A significant number of Filipinos are buying it.  

This pattern is not unique to us. When leaders frame accountability as political attacks, it creates solidarity with supporters. The base does not carefully weigh evidence. They close ranks around the person they feel is being unfairly targeted.  

Sara's camp has heavily leaned into this. The impeachment? Political. The funds investigation? Political. The cases against her father? Political. 

Everything is traced back to the Marcos administration going after their family.  

In Mindanao, that story takes hold. A February 2025 WR Numero survey showed 66% of Mindanao respondents opposed her impeachment, with only 17% in favor.  

Then her father got arrested.  

In March 2025, former President Rodrigo Duterte was brought to the International Criminal Court over the drug war. 

That created the exact picture her supporters needed: the Duterte family, seen as champions of the ordinary Filipino, being dismantled by domestic and international forces working together. 

Analysts pointed out that the image of a detained Duterte could trigger sympathy votes. The surveys suggest they were right.

🟥 MARCOS IS LOSING THE TRUST HE NEVER FULLY EARNED  

The other side of Sara's numbers is Marcos's decline. His satisfaction rating has been sliding since mid-2024, from +47 in December 2023 to -3 in November 2025. His disapproval reached 48% by December 2025, with only 34% approval.  

What we are seeing now feels like the real Bongbong Marcos without the Duterte machinery propping him up. 

This is why, at least from where I sit, he had to court the Dutertes in 2022 and agree to that UniTeam setup: him as president, Sara as vice. 

Without their base and their machinery, his numbers could not hold on their own. 

The cruel joke is on the Dutertes too. They ignored what their gut should have warned them about: that one day, they would end up one-upped by a Marcos. 

And why not? A Marcos is still a Marcos. The appetite for power and advantage has never really had a limit. But I digress. 

Going back, I see three big drivers.  

First, his anti-corruption push looks selective and weak. 

When he removed Sara from the National Security Council in January 2025 and called the role “irrelevant,” a lot of people read that as political, not governance-related. His handling of the flood control corruption scandal during the July 2025 SONA also damaged credibility. He pointed fingers while his own administration faced transparency questions.  

Second, the kitchen table issues. 

A February 2025 WR Numero survey showed his satisfaction had dropped to 30.1%, a 15-point fall from September 2024. Inflation, rising prices, job worries, daily survival. Sara might have misused millions in confidential funds, but regular Filipinos are struggling to pay for food and bills. They blame Marcos for that, not Duterte.  

Third, the regional picture is ugly for Marcos. 

He scored -37 in Mindanao, -7 in Visayas, and -8 in Metro Manila. Only Balance Luzon gave him a positive +18. Sara has a fortress. Marcos has a fragile patch of support in one broad region.  

🟥 THE SWS METHODOLOGY QUESTION: SAMPLE OR REALITY?  

The issue with these surveys isn't the figures alone, but the way they're cooked up and turned into ammunition. 

A December 2024 SWS survey on Sara's impeachment drew heavy criticism. 

One key point raised was that 55% of respondents were not even aware of the impeachment complaint, yet headlines still screamed that “41% of Filipinos support impeachment.” 

That raises a fair question. Should surveys highlight opinions on issues most respondents do not know about?  

The 2025 Senate race surveys added more pressure. 

SWS and Pulse Asia did not always agree. Some candidates underperformed their polling numbers on election day. Others did much better. This left many people wondering whether surveys were capturing real sentiment or simply reflecting name recall and visibility.  

SWS uses a sample of around 1,200 respondents, spread across Metro Manila, Balance Luzon, Visayas, and Mindanao, with face-to-face interviews. The national margin of error is about ±3%, and ±6% for each region. That is consistent with standard survey practice.  

But in a country with this level of regional political loyalty, where one respondent can statistically stand in for more than a dozen barangays, it is fair to ask if a sample of 1,200 can capture what over 110 million people really feel.  

I also have to admit my bias here. 

We live in a country where surveys can be commissioned, timed, and promoted by people who need a specific story in the headlines. 

Even experts still argue about this: do surveys just measure what people already think, or do they quietly push voters in a certain direction? 

We have Supreme Court decisions protecting firms that do not disclose who pays for what, and we have new proposals trying to regulate pre-election surveys more tightly. 

None of that cancels out how results are actually used during campaigns, in media, and in propaganda. 

Knowing that, and knowing how easily “mind conditioning” happens when the same numbers flash on TV and social feeds over and over, I take every survey result with a grain of salt.  

There is also a deeper question. Are these surveys measuring stable political attitudes, or just capturing emotions that are being pushed and pulled in real time by social media, political drama, troll farms, and elite messaging?

🟥 MINDANAO'S FORTRESS: WHEN SUPPORT STOPS MOVING  

The most striking part of the November 2025 SWS survey is the regional breakdown. 

Sara Duterte scored +74 in Mindanao, +43 in Visayas, +12 in Balance Luzon, and just +2 in Metro Manila.  

Her national rating looks moderate. Her Mindanao rating does not.  

And it barely moves. In February 2025, Pulse Asia showed her at 96% approval in Mindanao. By December 2025, she was still at 95%. 

Impeachment, corruption allegations, even accusations of threatening the President's life have not brought those numbers down.  

To me, this looks less like simple political support and more like identity. 

For many in Mindanao, especially Davao, the Duterte name has become tied to regional pride and dignity. 

An attack on Sara can feel like an attack on Mindanao. It is pakikisama and regional loyalty mixed together.  

Mindanao is about 23% of the Philippine population. If you hold that region almost completely, you can survive very ugly national headlines.  

🟥 THE SYMPATHY VOTE: HOW PERSECUTION TURNS INTO POPULARITY  

You see this in other countries too. When people feel their leader is being treated unfairly, they usually do not walk away. They hold on tighter.  

Look again at Sara's situation. Her father was arrested and sent to face charges abroad. Her own impeachment cases piled up. The first impeachment was invalidated by the Supreme Court. The Marcos administration appeared to be targeting both father and daughter in parallel. 

If you already like the Dutertes, this does not look like accountability. It looks like a coordinated takedown.  

The April 2025 Pulse Asia survey caught this shift. Despite her father's arrest and the impeachment moves, Sara's approval rating rose from 52% in February to 59% in March. Her trust numbers climbed from 53% to 61%. She said herself that she was surprised people still trusted her after all the attacks from politicians and social media.  

The more she was attacked, the more her base defended her.  

I am not saying the cases against her are weak or strong. I am saying the political effect of those cases, on her supporters, has been to make her look like a political martyr.

That is, if the surveys are real and not manipulated. 

🟥 WHAT THE SURVEY REALLY SHOWS: ONE COUNTRY, MANY REALITIES  

When I zoom out from the personalities, the November 2025 SWS survey does not tell me that “Filipinos love Sara” or “Filipinos hate Marcos.” It tells me we are living in different realities depending on where we are, how old we are, and how much education we have.  

Geographically, Metro Manila and Balance Luzon look more open to Marcos, while Mindanao and much of Visayas are firmly in Duterte territory. These are not small differences. These are opposing readings of who is legitimate and who is abusive.  

Urban and rural gaps are widening. Marcos's rating dropped 13 points in urban areas to -14, while it rose 17 points in rural areas to +9. People closer to dense media environments and national political coverage are reacting differently from those in places with fewer information sources.  

Age splits are glaring. Older Filipinos, 55 and above, gave Marcos positive scores around +15. Younger voters, 18-24, rated him at -14, and those aged 35-44 went even lower at -22.  

Education cuts deep. Respondents with no formal education gave Marcos a positive rating. College-educated Filipinos gave him a negative one. They are looking at the same President and drawing opposite conclusions.  

These divides were already there under Duterte and before that. The Marcos-Duterte breakup did not create them. It exposed them and made them harder to ignore.

🟥 THE DEMOCRACY PROBLEM HIDING INSIDE THE NUMBERS  

Here is the part that worries me most.  

If a politician's satisfaction rating can rise while standing in the middle of credible allegations of fund misuse, threats against the President, and multiple impeachment attempts, what does that say about how our accountability systems connect with public opinion?

A lot of work on voters’ behavior points in the same direction: if people see a leader as “isa sa atin,” they will forgive a lot. 

For Sara's Mindanao base, alleged misuse of confidential funds weighs less than her posture against what they see as Imperial Manila and its elites.  

If the numbers are accurate, we have to face a harder theory. Many Filipinos are not just victims of corruption from a distance. 

Many are willing to live with it when it comes from leaders they feel are on their side, especially if they believe they are getting protection, services, or a sense of pride in return. 

That does not mean Filipinos like corruption as an idea. It means corruption becomes acceptable background noise when loyalty and survival feel more important than clean governance.  

That mindset turns every legal case into just another political weapon. Impeachment looks like a trapo move, not a constitutional tool.

When 66% of Filipinos say she should face the Senate to answer the charges, but many of them also think she is being singled out unfairly, it shows how messy the line between justice and vendetta has become in our minds.  

SWS and other firms are trying to measure all of this. 

When they report that a big chunk of people support impeachment even if more than half had not heard of the complaint in detail, they are not necessarily wrong. 

They are showing us how loosely many of us hold political opinions. Often based on team colors and shared resentment, not on reading the actual evidence.  

🟥 DOES THE SURVEY MAKE SENSE?  

So, does this latest SWS survey make sense? On the technical side, probably yes. The sample, the margins of error, the fieldwork methods, all look consistent with long-standing practice in Philippine polling.  

But the political reality behind those numbers is messy.  

Sara Duterte's rising satisfaction amid impeachment and scandal makes sense if you understand how victim framing works as a political tool, how regional strongholds shield leaders, how Marcos's own credibility problems create room for her to pose as the “authentic” voice, and how sympathy votes solidify once supporters feel their leader is under siege.  

The part that does not sit well is what it says about us. It points to a political culture where serious allegations can still boost support, where legal processes start to look like weapons instead of safeguards, and where regions and classes live inside different fact worlds, consuming different feeds, and choosing leaders based not on performance but on perceived tribe.

The survey, in that sense, is not the villain. It is a mirror.

The question is, if this is already how badly we see truth and justice, how can we still expect elections and surveys to fix a problem that runs much deeper than the numbers?

 🟥 Sources:

🟥 THE FAKE “DEBATE CHAMPION” OF THE WEST PHILIPPINE SEA

[An MCT Opinion]

He’s not just a bad senator on the issue; he’s a bad debater on it—and that’s a different level of problem.

Rodante Marcoleta’s latest performance says everything you need to know about his so‑called debating skills.

Faced with Jay Tarriela’s invitation to actually see the situation in Kalayaan—join a flight, look at the waters, look at the risks and the harassment at sea—he declined the real‑world evidence and instead demanded a stage. 

He “dared” Tarriela, former Senior Associate Justice Antonio Carpio, and maritime law scholar Jay Batongbacal to a “friendly debate” on the West Philippine Sea.

Carpio, being Carpio, said fine. Let’s debate. He even suggested a clean, lawyerly agenda:  

1) Does the West Philippine Sea exist as a legal concept?  

2) Are the features around Kalayaan and Pag‑asa part of it?

On the surface, it looks like the classic “I’m not afraid to debate anyone” posture. 

Underneath, it exposes the core problem: 

Marcoleta is trying to drag settled positions of the Philippine state into a spectacle he’s not intellectually equipped to handle. 

And if it ever happens, the damage won’t be to his ego; it will be to the country’s legal and diplomatic position.

Because here’s the crucial truth: this is not a story about a brave senator who loves debate. This is about a politician who does not understand what debate actually is.

🟥 WHAT REAL DEBATE LOOKS LIKE (AND WHY HE DOESN’T MEET THE STANDARD)

Before we dissect his moves, we need a proper benchmark. You can’t call someone a bad debater if you never define what good looks like.

RAUL CONTRERAS: ONE PARAGRAPH, ZERO WASTED WORDS

In the 1950s, a 15‑year‑old Filipino student named Raul Contreras stood on an international stage and delivered an answer to the question “What is prejudice?” that people are still sharing today.

He didn’t rant. He didn’t posture. He defined prejudice as judging people not as individuals but by race, creed, or social status, then showed why that judgment is morally and practically wrong. 

In a short, composed response, he:

- Defined his central term precisely.  
- Built a logical progression from definition to consequences.  
- Anchored everything in a clear moral stance.

No theatrics. No fake bravado. Just disciplined thinking and clean language. 

That is what basic debating skill looks like in its purest form.

MODERN FILIPINO CHAMPIONS: HOW REAL DEBATERS ACTUALLY WIN

Fast‑forward to today: Filipino debaters from Ateneo, UP, and other universities have been winning or breaking at the World Universities Debating Championship and major Asian tournaments. 

They don’t win because they shout the loudest or read the most dramatic “manifestos.” They win because they:

- Define the motion: They clarify what the debate is really about and what counts as a win.  

- Frame the issues: They rank which questions matter most and focus there.  

- Use evidence properly: They bring facts, jurisprudence, and examples that actually support their claims.  

- Refute directly: They attack the strongest part of the other side’s case, not some strawman slogan.  

- Stay composed and flexible: They concede small points when necessary and adjust their line to fit the evidence, not the other way around.

That’s the standard: definition, framing, evidence, refutation, composure.

Now hold that against Rodante Marcoleta.

🟥 WHY THE WPS “FRIENDLY DEBATE” SHOULD NEVER HAPPEN

The clearest proof of Marcoleta’s lack of debating skill is precisely this show he’s trying to mount.

THE SET‑UP: ELEVATING GARBAGE TO “DEBATABLE”

The West Philippine Sea, as used in Philippine law, policy, and diplomacy, refers to the maritime areas in the South China Sea where the Philippines has sovereign rights under UNCLOS and the 2016 arbitral award. 

That includes waters around Pag‑asa and other features near Kalayaan. 

This is not some random nickname Filipinos made up for clout; it’s anchored in our own administrative issuances and reinforced by international law.

Marcoleta’s “debate challenge” is built on calling those very foundations into question. His line, in essence, is:

- Is there really such a thing as the West Philippine Sea?  

- Are we sure these waters and features actually fall under it?  

- And if not, are we “blowing things up” with our rhetoric and actions?

That sounds “brave” only if you ignore what’s already settled. 

When you drag the existence of the WPS and the scope of our rights into a “friendly debate,” you’re not just having a nice academic chat. You’re sending a signal to Beijing and the world that even Filipino leaders think this is up for discussion.

A real debater understands that not every “side” deserves a stage. 

Some positions are so weak, or so aligned with an aggressor’s propaganda, that putting them on equal footing with carefully built legal doctrine is a mistake. 

That’s Debate 101. Marcoleta either doesn’t see it—or doesn’t care.

LACSON AND TARRIELA: THE ADULTS IN THE ROOM

Look at the reactions around him.

Panfilo Lacson, hardly a bleeding‑heart dove, warned that a “showdown” like this could be agonizing, pointless, and harmful—not just to the legal argument, but to the residents of Pag‑asa whose rights and safety are at stake. 

He understands the trap: once you let a senator perform contrarian takes on settled rights in a “debate” with Carpio and Batongbacal, you gain nothing doctrinal and you hand China a talking point.

Jay Tarriela, for his part, pushed back by insisting that the WPS and our arbitral victory are not casual topics for public debate in the sense Marcoleta is pushing. 

They are obligations under the Constitution and international law, and his job is to defend them, not to pretend they’re still up for negotiation to entertain a senator’s thirst for attention and desire tonuse his position to show loyalty to Beijing.

In other words: the people who actually carry the burden of defending the Philippines at sea and in court are saying no, this is not a game show.

THE PROPAGANDA GIFT TO CHINA

If this “friendly debate” goes ahead, you already know the headlines and talking points that Chinese state media and proxies will run with:

- “Filipino senator questions existence of ‘West Philippine Sea’.”  
- “Philippine experts divided on scope of sea claims.”  
- “Even Filipinos admit the issue is not settled.”

They don’t need to win on logic. They just need enough clips of a Philippine senator, on stage with Carpio and Batongbacal, treating our own rights as something you can take or leave depending on who argues better on TV.

That is the opposite of what a responsible, skilled debater does. 

A real debater knows that the first question is not “Am I brave enough to debate?” It’s “Is this a motion that should be debated at all, given the stakes and the existing body of law?”

Marcoleta fails that basic question. And that’s exactly why, if this “debate” happens, it will not be a triumph of free speech. 

It will be yet another episode of Philippine officials doing China’s dirty narrative work for them, under the guise of “just asking questions”—led by a man who doesn’t actually know how to debate.
63:
🟥 DO YOU WANT WAR?

[An MCT Commentary]

Sen. Rodante Marcoleta floated a thought that landed like surrender disguised as caution: that the Kalayaan Island Group might not be worth the risk of conflict. He later said people misunderstood him, but the public backlash didn’t come from nowhere. People heard a senator treating Philippine territory like a bargaining chip.

Malacañang answered the way a government should answer that kind of talk. Palace Press Officer Claire Castro said the President’s position stays the same: no part of our territory or interests will be surrendered to any foreign entity. She also added a legal clarification that’s worth getting right: treason, by law, requires an overt act of war, so those remarks don’t meet that threshold.

That legal detail doesn’t let anyone off the hook. It just forces us to name the real problem accurately.

What we’re looking at isn’t treason in court, but a deep ethical failure. 

And the weapon used to protect that failure is a question that keeps getting recycled by the Marcoletas and the DDS machine.

Do you want war?

🟥 TWO MARCOLETAS, ONE SCRIPT

There are two Marcoletas in this story, and mixing them up is how people get confused.

The father is Sen. Rodante Marcoleta, the one tied to the “give up the KIG” line that triggered Malacañang’s no-surrender response.

The son is Rep. Paolo Henry Marcoleta, who drew backlash after remarks framed around putting people “in the frontlines” if China attacks, including comments that led to the “cannon fodder” outrage.

Different positions. Different moments.

Same emotional script.

The script forces the conversation into fear.

🟥 WHAT THE LAW ALREADY SAYS (AND WHY IT MATTERS)

One reason the “Do you want war?” line irritates me is that it acts like we’re choosing between two buttons: surrender or war.

We’re not.

In 2016, the tribunal in the South China Sea arbitration concluded there was no legal basis for China to claim “historic rights” to resources within the sea areas inside the nine-dash line beyond what UNCLOS allows. That ruling is not a magic shield, but it should concern us because it gives the Philippines a legal foundation for asserting rights without firing a shot.

Law is part of avoiding war.

So when someone asks, “Do you want war?” while dismissing or weakening the legal position, it starts to feel like sabotage spun as concern.

🟥 WHAT TARRIELA SAID THAT HIT ME

I listened to Jay Tarriela earlier, and his point was almost painfully simple: why are we even debating this.

Not debating as in “we can’t disagree about tactics.” He meant something more basic. When it comes to the West Philippine Sea, we’re supposed to have one stand: fight for the Philippines.

That’s why he pushed back hard when Marcoleta tried to turn sovereignty into a “friendly debate.”

Sen. Rodante Marcoleta challenged Tarriela to a “friendly debate,” and he even named retired Justice Antonio Carpio and UP maritime law expert Jay Batongbacal as people who should be included.

Tarriela didn’t mince words: our territorial integrity and legal entitlements in the West Philippine Sea aren’t a topic for public debate, and framing them as “debatable” risks undermining the oath a senator takes to uphold the Constitution and laws.

If you are a senator of the Republic, why are you acting like the Republic’s rights are just one opinion among many?

🟥 THE SENATE FLOOR MOMENT THAT PROVES THE POINT

Then the Senate floor itself became a live demo of the same distraction.

Sen. Kiko Pangilinan and Sen. Alan Peter Cayetano clashed during interpellation on Pangilinan’s resolution condemning remarks by the Chinese Embassy against Philippine government officials. 

The discussion narrowed to optics, specifically Commodore Tarriela’s use of a caricature of Xi Jinping and whether that was appropriate. 

Cayetano pushed it into a personal-direction hypothetical, saying that if that’s the stand, then his staff can show a meme of Pangilinan too, and the session was suspended twice.

This is how it happens.

The spotlight slides from what China is doing to how Filipinos are supposed to behave while China does it.

We end up arguing about tone, memes, and “professionalism.”

Meanwhile, the actual problem keeps moving in the water.

I’m just glad there are still senators like Kiko Pangilinan pushing back against the clownery that flirts with betrayal.

🟥 WHATABOUTISM AND DEFLECTION, FILIPINO STYLE

This is where “Do you want war?” becomes a dirty trick.

Whataboutism is a tactic where someone responds to a difficult accusation or question by raising a different issue or making a counteraccusation, instead of dealing with the original point.

So instead of answering “why are you floating surrender,” the conversation gets redirected to “so you want Filipino lives lost?”

Instead of answering “why are you undermining our position,” the conversation gets redirected to “so you want to provoke China?”

Call it what it is: A PRESSURE TACTIC. 

It forces you to defend your humanity before you can defend your country.

🟥 WHY THIS DRIFTS INTO PRO-CHINA RHETORIC

People get angry when you say “pro-China rhetoric.” They insist it’s just realism.

But look at the effect.

When surrender talk is normalized, China benefits. When Filipinos are trained to police their own voices, China benefits. When the public is told that asserting rights is “warmongering,” China benefits.

That’s why warnings about propaganda should exist in the first place.

In late 2025, West Philippine Sea advocates and observers warned that China’s “humanitarian” moment involving a distressed Filipino fisherman functioned as propaganda and image management, and the article notes how Chinese Embassy messaging gained traction partly because it was amplified by DDS personalities.

That’s the ecosystem.

Fear, distraction, and moral shaming, repeated until Filipinos start doubting themselves.

🟥 WHERE I STAND ON THIS

I accept the legal point: this isn’t treason under the strict penal definition being discussed publicly.

But moral language exists for a reason. A leader can avoid legal liability and still betray the public trust.

When elected officials float surrender and then hide behind “peace,” they’re not protecting Filipino lives. They’re protecting their own comfort. They’re protecting the right to be unchallenged.

And when Congress gets pulled into fights about caricatures while China keeps testing our limits, it tells you how fragile our national attention has become.

Our biggest vulnerability is not just ships and missiles.

It’s how easily powerful people can drag us away from sovereignty and into noise.

So when they ask, “Do you want war?”

I hear the real question underneath it.

Do you still want leaders who are more afraid of angering China than of failing the Philippines?
64:
🟥 WHEN A SENATOR PLAYS WITH YOUR NAME  

[An MCT Reflection]

On February 7, Nutribun Republic posted a public statement saying a sitting senator had “nearly doxxed” the person behind the page on national television.  

By the time I went looking for the clip people were talking about, the video had already been taken down.  

What I have now are Nutribun’s own words and the reactions of people who watched it while it was still up: a senator on TV, hinting that he knows who runs the page, offered as his answer to criticism.  

There’s a special kind of arrogance when a lawmaker uses his platform to flex that he knows your identity but refuses to answer the issue you raised.  

That’s what happened to Nutribun Republic, at least based on the written record that’s left.  

A sitting lawmaker, with all the powers and protections of the institution behind him, decided that instead of disproving Nutribun’s posts, he’d flirt with doxxing the person behind the page.  

He didn’t bring evidence.  

He brought a warning.  

You can see it in the way people described it: “nearly doxxed,” “power-tripping,” “on national television.”  

Hindi niya sinabi nang diretso ang buong pangalan, pero malinaw ang mensahe: “Alam namin kung sino ka.”  

And if you’ve watched how this lawmaker behaves over the years, it fits.  

He has never handled criticism well.  

🟥 WHO NUTRIBUN REALLY THREATENS  

Nutribun Republic is not some anonymous troll farm with ten followers and a stock profile picture.  

It is a long-running page with hundreds of thousands of people watching, fact-checking, and pushing back against lies.  

I’ve watched Nutribun get hit before.  
Mass reporting.  
Troll swarms.  
People throwing random names around, hoping one will stick.  

The page has survived coordinated reporting and earlier attempts to expose the identity of the person behind it with fake names and invented scandals.  

And yet Nutribun is still there, still posting, and still managing to annoy the kind of politician who thinks criticism is a crime.  

That’s the real threat Nutribun poses.  
Not violence.  
Not destabilization.  
Just receipts.  

🟥 MARCOLETA’S LONG HISTORY OF PUNISHING CRITICS  

This is where, for me, the “of course he did this” feeling comes in.  

Even without the clip, I don’t have to imagine his style from scratch.  

We’ve seen versions of it before.  

He was front and center when the House voted to slash the Commission on Human Rights budget to 1,000 pesos, then complained later about being “bashed” for it.  

He has used hearings and public platforms to cry about “misinformation” and “attacks” instead of answering what people are actually asking him.  

In one hearing, he went after a lawyer-vlogger who warned viewers not to be fooled by his theatrics.  

Again, instead of tackling the substance, he focused on the critic.  

So when I read people describing how he “nearly doxxed” Nutribun Republic on national television, it didn’t feel like a wild accusation.  

It felt like the latest entry in a long pattern: when criticized, he doesn’t fix the issue, he goes after the person talking.  

Now we’re here.  
A watchdog page posts about him.  
He responds by almost exposing the person behind it on national TV.  

With him, the move is familiar: he doesn’t fix the issue, he goes after the person who talks about it.  

🟥 WHY ANONYMITY IS A HUMAN RIGHTS ISSUE  

Anonymity, in this country, is not a gimmick.  
It’s a safety measure.  

Most people who run political pages are not oligarchs.  
They are employees, freelancers, parents, caregivers, call center agents, teachers.  
They have bosses.  
They have families.  
They have rent.  

I know the person behind Nutribun Republic.  
I’ve seen what it costs to keep posting despite threats, and Nutribun still chooses to show up.  

When a senator hints that he knows your name, your workplace, maybe even where your kid studies, the message is simple: you are being warned, not informed.  

The message to the rest of us is simple:  
Post about corruption and we might drag your offline life into it.  

And this is coming from someone who helped gut the budget of an institution meant to protect citizens from state abuse.  
The irony writes itself.  

🟥 THE SUPPORT AROUND NUTRIBUN  

One thing I’ve learned following pages like Nutribun is that the harassment is real, but so is the support.  

When the near-doxxing happened, people didn’t just shrug.  
There were threads, outrage posts, and statements calling it out as an abuse of power.  

Even former COA commissioner Heidi Mendoza and other respected figures voiced support for Nutribun in the comments.  

That support matters.  
Because if public officials see that you can threaten an anonymous critic and nobody reacts, they will do it again.  
And next time, it won’t be “nearly doxxed.”  

🟥 AT THE END OF THE DAY, YOU’RE ALONE  

There’s something else people don’t see when they share statements of support or quote big names defending you.  
When the doxxing threat feels real, you still face it alone.  

Yes, there are posts, threads, and kind words.  
But the person who has to worry about the doorbell, the school pickup, the HR memo, the late-night unknown number, is you.
  
What happened to Nutribun is already a form of threat, whether or not the full name was read out.  

The whole point is to make you imagine what could happen next if they decide to push harder.  

Lawmaker statements?  

Most of them won’t touch a case like this unless there’s clout or cameras involved.  

The law that should protect you can also be flipped and used against you, repackaging criticism as defamation, disinformation, or “threats to public order.”  

So if push comes to shove, you don’t really get a cavalry.  
You get your own spine, whatever savings you have, maybe a lawyer friend if you’re lucky, and a decision: do I keep going or do I shut this down?  

That’s the part that’s hard to explain to people who only see the page, not the person behind it.  

🟥 THIS IS BIGGER THAN ONE PAGE  

This started with one blogger and one senator.  
It now raises a bigger question: what kind of country are we building when elected officials feel entitled to peek behind anonymity and flex that information on TV?  

Today it’s Nutribun Republic.  
Tomorrow it can be a teacher who posts about underfunded classrooms.  
A nurse talking about hospital shortages.  
A government employee pointing out procurement anomalies.  

If they can turn your Facebook page into an excuse to dig into your personal life, that chills everyone watching.  

And if this is already the climate now, imagine the kind of pressure anonymous critics would face in a government led by someone like Sara Duterte, backed by the same troll ecosystems and tough-on-critics mindset we’ve seen from the Duterte brand for years.  

I’m not shocked that it was Marcoleta who did this.  

His record on critics and accountability has been consistent for years, and not in a good way.  

I don’t know how long pages like Nutribun can keep absorbing hits like this.  

But I know who should be scared of that question, and it’s not the anonymous blogger.
65:
🟥 Marcoleta Wants Us to Give Up Islands. Batongbacal Just Explained Why That's National Suicide.

I just finished watching Atty. Jay Batongbacal's explanation (Via the Ted Failon and DJ Chacha morning show) on the Kalayaan Island Group issue, and I have to say — the contrast between him and Senator Rodante Marcoleta is night and day.

Batongbacal is a maritime affairs expert. He is a professor of international maritime law at the University of the Philippines and has dedicated decades of his career to studying sovereignty, exclusive economic zones, and the Philippines' legal position in the West Philippine Sea. 

When he speaks on these matters, he does so with precision, with legal grounding, and with an understanding of what is actually at stake.

His argument is simple and devastating: if the Philippines gives up the Kalayaan Island Group just because China is applying pressure, we set a dangerous precedent. 

One that tells Beijing — and the rest of the world — that all it takes to carve out Philippine territory is enough intimidation. 

Once that door opens, it doesn't close. 

If we surrender KIG today because we're afraid of confrontation, what stops China from escalating in Palawan next? In Zambales? In Batanes? 

Are we going to keep handing over pieces of the country every time Beijing throws a tantrum?

That is the core of Batongbacal's warning: the precedent is the real threat.

Now compare that to Marcoleta.

On February 4, 2026, during a Commission on Appointments hearing, Marcoleta said — and these are his own words — "Ano ba talaga ipagpapatayan natin dito? We will offer our lives, including the lives of our children, to die for it for something that is not even within our EEZ. Ang gawin po natin para madali, i-give up natin ang KIG."

Mantakin mo iyon? A sitting senator just told the Filipino people to surrender their own territory for convenience. 

He later backpedaled, claiming people were "abbreviating the context" of his remarks. 

But the video is there. The transcript is there. Nobody abbreviated anything.

And this is not the first time. Let's trace the pattern:

- In January 2026, Marcoleta relayed Chinese Ambassador Huang Xilian's complaints that China was being portrayed as the "kontrabida" in the West Philippine Sea, practically functioning as a mouthpiece for Beijing inside the Philippine Senate. 

- He suggested the Philippines should consider sharing the West Philippine Sea with China — as if sovereign territory is a potluck.

- Back when he was still a congressman in 2025, Marcoleta claimed the West Philippine Sea "does not exist" and is merely "a creation by us" — a statement so absurd that even President Marcos dismissed it as headline-grabbing.

- He questioned the government's transparency initiative that documents Chinese incursions, warning it could cause "panic" — essentially telling the government to stop exposing what China is doing.

- And when called out, his response was to suggest that Commodore Jay Tarriela and "arrogant senators" be placed on the front line of war — literally wishing harm on people defending the country.

Now here is the argument Marcoleta keeps falling back on: war. "Do you want war with China?" 

That is the rhetorical hammer he swings every single time. And it is the laziest, most intellectually dishonest argument in Philippine geopolitics today.

Nobody — not Batongbacal, not Tarriela, not Hontiveros, not Lacson — is calling for war. 

What they are calling for is the enforcement of international law. 

The 2016 Arbitral Tribunal ruling overwhelmingly sided with the Philippines, categorically rejecting China's nine-dash line and affirming Philippine sovereign rights in its exclusive economic zone. 

That ruling is the strongest legal weapon a small nation has ever wielded against a superpower. 

And Marcoleta wants us to throw it away because he's scared of hypothetical consequences.

This "war" rhetoric is not original. It is a recycled Duterte-era talking point. 

Rodrigo Duterte used the exact same framing for six years: "We cannot afford to go to war with China." 

Nobody was asking for war. But that line was repeated so many times that it became the default excuse for inaction, for appeasement, for looking the other way while China built artificial islands, harassed Filipino fishermen, and water-cannoned Philippine vessels in our own waters.

Marcoleta is not thinking independently. He is running the same playbook.

Senate President Pro Tempore Ping Lacson put it best: giving up the Kalayaan Island Group is no different from surrendering Luzon — or the entire country. 

The KIG was incorporated into Philippine territory under Presidential Decree No. 1596 in 1978. 

Kalayaan is a municipality of Palawan. Around 300 Filipino families live on Pag-asa Island. 

These are not abstract dots on a map. These are Filipino communities, Filipino soil, Filipino waters.

Dr. Jose Antonio Goitia, chairman emeritus of several civic defense groups, said it plainly: "This is how nations lose territory without firing a shot. They surrender the argument before it is even heard."

That is exactly what Marcoleta is doing. 

He is surrendering the argument. And whether he does it out of cowardice, ignorance, or something else entirely — the effect is the same. It weakens the Philippines. It emboldens China. And it tells every Filipino fisherman in the West Philippine Sea that their own government doesn't think they're worth defending.

When I first heard Marcoleta's argument, the first thing I blurted out was — this man has zero love for this country. 

On his TV show, he talks tough. He performs patriotism. 

But if you follow his actual positions — his relay of Chinese ambassador talking points, his denial of the West Philippine Sea, his call to surrender KIG, his attacks on the very officials defending our sovereignty — the pattern is unmistakable.

The argument of war is the argument of a man who has already decided to surrender. 

It is the argument of someone who sees a hammer and thinks every problem is a nail. 

It is lazy. It is dishonest. And it has been the pro-China line in the Philippines since 2016.

Batongbacal gave us the counter-argument grounded in law, sovereignty, and long-term strategic thinking. Marcoleta gave us fear masquerading as pragmatism.

I know which one I'm listening to. 🇵🇭
66:
🟥 PAYING FOR NOTHING: THE GRIM REALITY OF A FILIPINO TAXPAYER

[Warning: long piece ahead. Try not to read it on a Sunday as it could potentially ruin your weekend mood.]

The Philippines just signed a P8.18 billion loan agreement with Japan for the MRT-3. Again.

Same train line. Third rehab loan.

The MRT-3 is 27 years old. It carries around 400,000 passengers a day and breaks down so often it feels normal. Instead of being kept in good condition, it keeps getting patched through new foreign loans.

That P8.18 billion isn’t for something new. It’s for something we already built, already borrowed for in the past, and let fall apart because nobody set aside money for maintenance. 

So this is the real bill we’re paying. Not just for services, but for the government’s failure to take care of what it already has. 

We’re paying interest on loans for things that broke because the people in charge don’t know how to save, plan, or maintain.

I'm not a budget expert. I'm not an economist. I'm a taxpayer reading COA audits and Senate transcripts. 

And what they're writing down—always failing to spend the budget, overpricing, missing funds—doesn't require expertise to recognize as failure.

But it needs a serious paying attention.

Before we even argue about waste, here's the part people forget: the payment is automatic.

Here is where this should bother you as it does me: 

You get deducted for taxes  even before you see your paycheck. 

Income tax kicks in once you cross P250,000 a year, starting at 15 percent. 

SSS takes a share too—as of January 2025, the total contribution rate is 15 percent. PhilHealth at 5 percent. 

Pag-IBIG at 2 percent. Then the bank taxes your savings interest at 20 percent. 

Every peso you spend carries 12 percent VAT.

This is supposed to be the deal: 

You pay taxes, the government delivers. Schools that work. Hospitals that don't bankrupt families. Roads that don't flood. Mass transit that runs. Programs that reach the people who need them.

Here's what you actually get: a third loan for a broken train. P545 billion spent on flood control that didn't stop the floods. Movable “extra” funds that magically become “priority” projects. And an entire government that keeps acting surprised when it can't deliver what it promised.

And we keep paying. Because we have no choice (I mean,compared to the rich ones who are supposed to appear in the BIR's list of taxpeyers but doesn't. 🤷‍♂️)

🟥 THE PATTERN (AND NO, IT DIDN'T START WITH MARCOS)

P545 billion for 9,855 flood control projects from 2022 to 2025. Manila still floods. The contractors got rich. Politicians got kickbacks. 

Between 2023 and 2025, as much as P1.089 trillion of the government's climate-labeled spending may have been lost to corruption.

That's not a typo. Over a trillion pesos.

It's tempting to treat this as a Marcos-only story because the MRT loan happened now, the 2026 budget fight is happening now, the flood-control scandal exploded now.

But if we're being honest, this pattern has been there for a long time.

Under Duterte's Build Build Build push, COA flagged something that should've been a national scandal on its own: in 2017, DPWH set aside around P610.9 billion for projects but paid out only P222.66 billion—about a third—because of delayed or non-implementation of projects.

We are not talking about kickbacks here yet.

Ang scandal diyan is the government failing to spend its own infrastructure money during the so‑called golden age of infrastructure.

And the flood control money pipe got big under Duterte too. 

A policy note later pointed out that flood control budgets went from around P79 billion in 2016 to over P200 billion a year by 2021.

The pipe got wider under Duterte, then stayed wide under Marcos.

The Department of Budget and Management wrote a report years ago that already showed this. 

Government spending in 2014 fell short by P302.7 billion—almost double the P140.9 billion shortfall in 2013, four times the P76.7 billion in 2012. 

By 2015, the shortfall hit P390.3 billion. 

On average, government failed to spend P210.6 billion a year.

The study showed the problem: built‑in problems in the system made up 62 percent of the failure. 

Nine agencies—DPWH, DepEd, DSWD, DILG, DA, DOH, DOTC, DAR, and DENR—were responsible for 46 percent of unspent funds.

The reasons: badly prepared programs and projects. Inability to do projects on schedule. Late billing from contractors. Problems in the bidding process.

Translation: the government has money. It just can't figure out how to spend it properly.

Then there's the opposite problem: when the government does spend, it wastes in a big way.

Private companies cut costs to maximize profit. DPWH does the opposite. 

Base costs for government work are 30 to 100 percent higher than private estimates. 

DPWH uses a “Bill of Materials,” which is just an internal working list, instead of a “Bill of Quantities,” which is a formal document that puts a licensed expert on the line.

The result: bloated budgets from the start. Hazard maps exaggerated to justify high costs. 

The “use it or lose it” rule pushing agencies to add useless work just to finish the money. Bidding where the winner shares with the losers.

Former Budget Secretary Amenah Pangandaman admitted in a hearing: DBM doesn't have enough people to check DPWH projects on the ground. 

Dig this: Checking projects is not even part of their official job.

So we fund. We borrow. We build. We can't check. We can't inspect.

🟥 HOW THE MONEY GETS CHOPPED UP

Senator Juan Miguel Zubiri laid out the pattern: politicians chop up flood control funds. 

Instead of putting P10 billion into one major project that actually solves flooding, the money gets scattered. 

Each district, each politician gets their own small deals. Dredging projects here. Small flood control there. Nothing you can clearly see working.

The result: no one big plan. No clear program. No proper spillways, floodways, water impounding systems, or pumping stations. Just thousands of scattered projects that don't connect to anything.

From 2011 to 2025, DPWH got P1.90 trillion for flood control—roughly P348 million a day.

I had to read that three times. P348 million. Every single day. For fourteen years.

And Manila still floods.

Senator Panfilo Lacson asked the right question: what happened to the huge amount?

Here's what happened: the money got divided among 602 agencies doing projects, each with its own built‑in problems.

Poor budget preparation. Inability to design projects that are ready to start. More and more lump‑sum funds. Bidding made harder by lack of clear project details.

DBM already showed this years ago too: agencies can’t finish using their budgets on time, so they drag projects into the next year and do it again and again.

This is how government wastes money: not just through stealing, but through sheer inability to plan, coordinate, or do the work properly.

🟥 THE UNPROGRAMMED FUNDS CIRCUS

Here's how the government pretends to be strict with money while still keeping lots of room to move it around: unprogrammed funds.

These are supposed to give government flexibility for emergencies or sudden needs.

In reality, they’re a movable pot of money that lawmakers and the Palace can use for whatever they call “priority.”

The 2026 budget originally included P243 billion in unprogrammed funds. 

After public anger over the flood control scandal, President Marcos vetoed P92.5 billion, bringing it down to P150 billion—“the absolute bare minimum,” according to Finance Secretary Ralph Recto.

But even that “bare minimum” is misleading. 

From 2023 to 2025, Marcos opened up P214 billion in unprogrammed funds for “priority” infrastructure under DPWH. Half of that—P111 billion—went to flood control.

The same flood control projects now under investigation for massive corruption.

And this kind of pot didn’t suddenly appear in 2022. 

Under Duterte, unprogrammed funds were already built into the budget; by 2022, Congress had already pushed that number higher than what his own DBM proposed.

The 2025 budget law was described by critics as one of the most flawed and corrupt spending laws ever passed. 

House Speaker Martin Romualdez, cousin of President Marcos, got P14.4 billion in “allocable” funds—the second‑highest in Congress.

A budget watchdog group calls these “allocable” funds a new kind of pork barrel—projects picked for politics that push out fair and transparent spending.

This is the pattern: money set aside for services gets pulled into pots that politicians control. 

PhilHealth subsidies cut completely—P74 billion gone. The 4Ps program cut by P50 billion, replaced by AKAP, where politicians decide who gets help.

Education cut. Higher education cut. Skills training cut. Agriculture cut. Labor cut. Social welfare cut.

Meanwhile, the pots politicians can move around keep growing.

🟥 WHY SERVICES KEEP DISAPPEARING

The money goes to infrastructure. Not because infrastructure is always the top need. Because infrastructure is where the freedom to move money lives.

The Build Better More program gets P1.556 trillion in 2026. 

Rail projects delayed for years. The Malolos‑Clark Railway moving in fits and starts. 

The Kaliwa Dam displacing Indigenous peoples. 

Solar farms in Ilocos Norte making heat and flooding worse for nearby communities.

When 76.7 percent of climate-labeled money goes to DPWH, 13 percent to the Department of Transportation, and only 1 percent to DENR, you're not really building the ability to handle climate disasters. You're just feeding the same big public works pipe.

Education, health, and social services don't offer the same room for patronage. 

You can't put your name on a classroom’s improved reading scores. You can't pose beside a working PhilHealth subsidy. You can't claim credit for a social program that quietly works and doesn’t promise votes.

But you can put your name on a bridge. You can  have a photo op when you cut a ribbon for a road. You can hand out cash with your face on the tarp.

So education funding stays below what UNESCO suggests (4 to 6 percent of GDP); the Philippines is at about 3.5 percent. 

Health is at about 1 percent of GDP, far from the 5 percent the WHO recommends. 

Safety nets get about 2.2 percent against the ILO’s 5.1 percent suggestion.

Meanwhile, DENR gets P29.3 billion—around 0.43 percent of the P6.79 trillion national budget. 

Solid waste management gets P349 million despite Manila’s garbage problem. 

The National Greening Program gets P1.66 billion and has failed since 2011 to properly reforest land with native trees.

The government isn't just bad at saving money. It's bad at choosing what to spend on. 

Visibility wins. Impact loses.

🟥 WHAT YOU'RE ACTUALLY PAYING FOR

The 2026 national budget is around P6.79 trillion. 

Debt payments—interest and paying back the borrowed money—take over P2 trillion. That’s roughly 30 percent just to pay for past decisions.

The rest gets sliced badly. 

DPWH: about P530 billion for 2026, lower than the over P1 trillion range only because the flood control scandal made that impossible to defend. 

Defense: about P310 billion. DILG: about P310 billion.

On paper, these amounts look decent. 

But here's what you don't see: agencies that matter often fail to spend what they have, while agencies that are good for kickbacks get more than they can use properly. 

And there's very weak ability to track where the money actually goes. 🤷‍♂️

The Commission on Audit checks after the money is gone, after projects fail, after contractors vanish. 

COA reported that officials and contractors in DPWH Bulacan may face graft charges over P360 million in flood control projects. 

That's one province. One audit.

The Anti‑Money Laundering Council had to freeze 135 bank accounts and 27 insurance policies linked to flood control corruption. 

That's clean‑up, not prevention.

Congress’ own budget research office even floated lowering VAT from 12 percent to 10 percent—meaning around P200 billion less revenue. 

That's still only a slice of what is lost to corruption and waste each year. 

If the government actually knew how to save money, that VAT cut would be possible.

They haven’t cut VAT. They’re not fixing how bidding works. They’re not simplifying spending. They’re not investing in maintenance.

They’re asking you to pay more while giving you less.

The taxes are real. Income tax. SSS at 15 percent. PhilHealth at 5 percent. Pag‑IBIG at 2 percent. VAT at 12 percent. The 20 percent tax on your savings interest.

The services are half‑real, half‑imaginary.

Schools that ask parents for basic supplies. Hospitals where out‑of‑pocket spending is around half of total health spending—one of the worst in ASEAN. 

Roads that flood every rainy season. Garbage piling up despite only P349 million for solid waste.

Government paying 30 to 100 percent more than the private sector would. 

Agencies repeatedly failing to spend over P200 billion a year. 

Movable pots of money turning into loose funds for politicians.

When government doesn’t know how to plan, buy, build, or check properly, corruption fills every gap.

Senator Sherwin Gatchalian warned that the remaining P150 billion in unprogrammed funds “should be guarded with great caution to avoid wasteful expenses including possible corruption.”

But guarding against waste needs people and tools the government doesn’t have. 

DBM itself says it doesn’t have enough people to inspect DPWH projects. 

COA checks after the damage. 

The bidding process is twisted from the start.

So, ang problema ng bansa is not just limited stealing. A huge part of this is a system that’s terrible with money to begin with—and that weakness becomes cover for theft.

🟥 WHAT THIS MEANS

Former Finance Secretary and now Executive Assistant Ralph Recto calls the slower government spending in Q3 2025 a “short‑term adjustment” for good governance. 

The promise: more efficient, transparent, accountable spending next.

That's always the promise.

But nothing in the 2026 budget shows that the government has really learned how to save or spend well. 

DPWH still gets over half a trillion pesos—cut only because scandal forced the issue. 

DENR still gets less than half a percent of the budget. 

Education, health, and safety nets still miss global recommendations.

The deep problems stay. 

Agencies that can’t use their budgets properly. 
Bidding systems that invite waste. Movable pots of money that act like pork barrel. 

A Commission on Audit that comes in after the theft. No serious way to check projects. No real punishment for repeat offenders.

Marcos can brag about vetoing P92.5 billion in unprogrammed funds, but P150 billion is still there, sitting in the same pot everyone already warned about.

The government doesn’t need to squeeze more from you first.

It needs to stop wasting what it already has. Pronto. 

Until then, every peso you pay in taxes is a gamble. 

Will it fund a service that works? Or will it pay for the third loan to fix a train that should have been taken care of from the start?

I started writing this thinking about the MRT. That third loan. That P8.18 billion borrowed because nobody planned for maintenance.

Now I'm looking at everything else. The flood control that didn't work. The constant failure to use the budget properly. The bidding games that never stop. The movable funds that keep getting bigger.

And I realize: the MRT isn't the exception.

It's the pattern.

Build something. Let it break. Borrow money to fix it. Repeat.

At some point, paying for nothing stops being a budget issue.

It becomes a trust issue.
